tree_title: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
tree_topic: Arts & Entertainment > Entertainment Industry > Film & TV Industry
overall_topic: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
questions: Within the framework of mastering the seven stages of film production—from development and financing through marketing and distribution—identify the producing school that explicitly trains students across the entire production pipeline in both creative and business capacities. Then, provide a comprehensive overview of: (1) the full range of degree programs it offers (including graduate and undergraduate options), (2) the locations where these programs are available, (3) the structure and focus of its 1-year certificate programs, (4) the different online workshop formats and their durations, including any industry-standard software taught, and (5) at least two faculty members and two notable alumni along with their professional roles. Your answer should synthesize information across these categories to demonstrate how the institution prepares producers to manage all seven stages of film production.

An American film school that offers producing degrees and workshops in New York, Los Angeles, and online emphasizes mastery of the full filmmaking pipeline—from development and financing through marketing and distribution. For this institution, outline the key provisions of its official digital web and mobile privacy policy. Specifically, explain how it handles personally identifiable information (including whether it is shared with third parties), what types of non-personally identifiable data it collects automatically, how cookies are used and managed, its approach to targeted advertising, the security measures it states it employs (including any mention of encryption), and the official contact method provided for privacy-related questions.

rounds.answer_a:
New York Film Academy’s Producing School is designed to train producers through all seven stages of the production pipeline—development, financing, pre-production, production, post-production, marketing, and distribution—balancing creative decision-making with business management [1].

1) Degree programs  
• Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Producing (4 semesters)  
• Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) in Producing (8 semesters)  
• Associate of Fine Arts (AFA) in Producing (4 semesters)  
• 2-Year Conservatory in Producing (non-degree)  
All degrees embed courses in script analysis, Movie Magic Scheduling & Budgeting, entertainment law, marketing, and distribution strategy [2][3].

2) Locations  
Degree and conservatory programs are offered on NYFA campuses in Los Angeles, New York City, and South Beach–Miami; the 1-year certificate is also available at Gold Coast, Australia, and Florence, Italy (summer intensive) [2][4].

3) 1-Year Certificate  
The 1-Year Producing Conservatory compresses the full curriculum into two semesters: Semester 1 focuses on story development, budgeting, and short-form production; Semester 2 moves to feature packaging, post workflows, festival strategy, and pitch deck creation, culminating in a capstone business plan and investor pitch [3].

4) Online workshops  
• 15-Week “Producer’s Craft” (nights/weekends)  
• 8-Week Intensive  
• 4-Week Bootcamp  
• 3-Week High School Summer  
All online formats combine live Zoom lectures with hands-on assignments in Movie Magic and Final Draft, delivering 30–90 contact hours depending on length [5].

5) Sample faculty & alumni  
Faculty:  
• Neal Weisman – Chair, Emmy-winning producer (The Politician’s Wife) [6]  
• Denise Carlson – Producer (Dragon Ball Z, Disney’s Mulan) [6]  

Notable alumni:  
• Aubrey Plaza – Actor/producer (Emily the Criminal) [7]  
• Bill Hader – Actor/producer (Barry, Saturday Night Live) [7]  

Through this comprehensive, multi-campus platform, NYFA equips aspiring producers to command every stage of a project from first pitch through worldwide release.  

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy – Producing School Overview.  
[2] NYFA Los Angeles Catalog – Producing Degree Programs.  
[3] NYFA One-Year Producing Conservatory Description.  
[4] NYFA International Campuses – Gold Coast & Florence.  
[5] NYFA Online Producing Workshops.  
[6] NYFA Faculty Bios – Producing Department.  
[7] NYFA Alumni Success Stories.
rounds.answer_b:
The school is **MetFilm School**, whose **Producing** pathway explicitly says students are trained to work “through all seven stages of production,” combining **creative, technical, and business** responsibilities needed to develop, finance, make, market, and distribute films.[1] Its degree portfolio spans **undergraduate and postgraduate study**: BA and accelerated **2-year** undergraduate degrees in areas including Filmmaking, Acting, Content, Screenwriting, Hair & Makeup, and practical business-facing pathways such as Producing; postgraduate options include **MA**, **MFA**, and **Postgraduate** programs in screen and creative disciplines.[2][3]

These programs are offered across multiple campuses: **London, Leeds, Berlin, and Brighton** in Europe, plus **MetFilm School Australia in Melbourne and Sydney**.[2][4] For focused upskilling, MetFilm’s **1-year certificate programs** are vocational, industry-led courses designed around intensive practical training in specialist screen fields, aimed at learners wanting a shorter pathway than a full degree while still building professional portfolios and production experience.[3]

Its **online workshops** come in several formats: **weekend labs/masterclasses**, **5-week evening courses**, and longer **8-week** or similar part-time options, with topics covering directing, producing, screenwriting, editing, and career skills.[5] Software training includes industry-standard tools such as **Avid Media Composer** and **Adobe Premiere Pro** in editing-focused courses.[5]

Faculty and industry mentors include **Jonny Persey** (director/co-founder, producer/exhibitor leader) and **Peter Webber** (film director of *Girl with a Pearl Earring*).[6][7] Notable alumni include **Sophie Odlum** (producer) and **Jack Barton** (writer-director).[8] Together, this ecosystem prepares producers to oversee the entire pipeline from package development and financing through physical production, post, marketing, sales, and release.[1][2]

## References
[1] MetFilm School, Producing course pages describing training across the seven stages of production: https://www.metfilmschool.ac.uk/  
[2] MetFilm School, Undergraduate and campus overview pages: https://www.metfilmschool.ac.uk/courses/  
[3] MetFilm School, Postgraduate and certificate program pages: https://www.metfilmschool.ac.uk/postgraduate/  
[4] MetFilm School Australia campus information: https://www.metfilmschool.edu.au/  
[5] MetFilm School, Online Courses / Short Courses pages: https://www.metfilmschool.ac.uk/short-courses/online-courses/  
[6] MetFilm School, Leadership/Faculty profiles for Jonny Persey: https://www.metfilmschool.ac.uk/about-us/  
[7] MetFilm School, Tutors/Industry mentors including Peter Webber: https://www.metfilmschool.ac.uk/tutors/  
[8] MetFilm School, Alumni pages: https://www.metfilmschool.ac.uk/alumni/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the New York Film Academy (NYFA) Producing School, which explicitly trains students across all seven stages of film production. Agent B instead selects MetFilm School, which does not match the Ground Truth entity. Therefore, Agent B fails the Core Entity Check (DEEP failure).

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers degree levels, locations, 1-year certificate structure, online workshops, software (Movie Magic), and faculty/alumni. However, it misses specific required items (e.g., MA/Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation – Online, 2-Day Line Producing Workshop, Jenni Powell, named producing alumni like Heidi Segal) and introduces likely inaccuracies (e.g., certain campuses and alumni). So Agent A has WIDE gaps and minor hallucinations. Agent B, although structured and detailed, aggregates information about the wrong institution, making its otherwise solid breadth irrelevant.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Both responses are clearly formatted with bullet points and citations. Agent A is well structured and directly aligned to the query framework. However, due to factual incompleteness, it cannot earn MUCH_BETTER. Since Agent B fails the main identity requirement, Agent A wins as the “Flawed Winner” (correct entity, imperfect detail coverage).

Thus, Agent A is BETTER, but not MUCH_BETTER due to sub-point inaccuracies.

For this institution, outline the key provisions of its official digital web and mobile privacy policy. Specifically, explain how it handles personally identifiable information (including whether it is shared with third parties), what types of non-personally identifiable data it collects automatically, how cookies are used and managed, its approach to targeted advertising, the security measures it states it employs (including any mention of encryption), and the official contact method provided for privacy-related questions.

rounds.answer_a:
New York Film Academy’s Digital Web & Mobile Privacy Policy states the following:

Personally Identifiable Information (PII)  
• NYFA collects PII voluntarily supplied through inquiry forms, applications, event RSVPs, e-commerce and email signup—e.g., name, postal address, e-mail, telephone, date of birth and payment data [1].  
• PII is used to provide requested services, process transactions, send institutional or marketing communications and improve programs.  
• It may be shared with “trusted third-party service providers” (hosting, payment processors, analytics, email platforms) bound to use it only for NYFA purposes, or disclosed when required by law; the policy states NYFA “does not sell” PII to unaffiliated third parties [1].

Non-PII Collected Automatically  
• When users visit NYFA sites or apps, the school logs browser type, device IDs, operating system, IP address, referring/exit pages, time stamps, pages viewed, clickstream data and general geolocation obtained through cookies, pixel tags, server logs and similar tech [1][2].

Cookies & Management  
• First-party and third-party cookies, web beacons and local storage are used to recognize repeat visitors, remember preferences, analyze traffic and measure campaign effectiveness.  
• Users can refuse or delete cookies via browser settings; links to opt-out tools (e.g., Google Analytics Opt-out, NAI/DAA AdChoices) are provided [1].

Targeted Advertising  
• NYFA participates in remarketing and interest-based advertising on Google, Facebook, Instagram and other ad networks; cookie data is matched to show ads that “may be of interest” to users. Opt-outs through network-specific tools or mobile device settings are described [1][2].

Security Measures  
• The Academy employs “administrative, technical and physical safeguards,” including firewalls, restricted access and Secure Socket Layer (SSL) encryption for transmission of sensitive data such as credit-card numbers. Despite these measures, it notes no system is completely secure [1].

Privacy Contact  
• Questions can be directed to: Privacy Officer, New York Film Academy, 17 Battery Place, 5th Floor, New York, NY 10004; e-mail privacy@nyfa.edu; phone +1 212-674-4300 [1].

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy – Digital Privacy Policy (nyfa.edu/legal/privacy-policy)  
[2] NYFA – Cookie & Ad Choices Section, same policy page
rounds.answer_b:
The institution is **New York Film Academy (NYFA)**, which offers producing study/workshops in **New York, Los Angeles, and online** and frames producer training across the full production pipeline.[1][2] Its official **Digital Web and Mobile Privacy Policy** says NYFA may collect **personally identifiable information** users voluntarily submit, such as names, contact details, and other information provided through inquiries, applications, or site interactions.[3] It states that such information may be shared with **third-party service providers** that help operate the website, process services, or support NYFA’s business functions, and may also be disclosed when required by law or to protect rights and safety.[3]

The policy also describes automatic collection of **non-personally identifiable information**, including items such as **IP address, browser type, device/mobile identifiers, operating system, pages viewed, referring/exit pages, dates/times of visits, and usage or clickstream data**.[3] NYFA says it uses **cookies and similar tracking technologies** to enhance site performance, remember preferences, analyze traffic, and support advertising/marketing functions.[3] Users can generally manage cookies through **browser settings**, although disabling them may affect some website functionality.[3]

On advertising, the policy indicates NYFA and advertising partners may use tracking tools to deliver **targeted or interest-based advertising** based on browsing behavior across websites or devices, with opt-out mechanisms available through browser controls or industry self-regulatory tools where applicable.[3]

For security, NYFA says it maintains **reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards** to protect data; however, it does not guarantee absolute security. The policy specifically notes that information submitted through the site may be transmitted using **encryption/SSL in certain areas**, while recognizing that no internet transmission is completely secure.[3] Privacy questions are directed to **privacy@nyfa.edu**.[3]

## References
[1] New York Film Academy, Producing School / locations and online offerings: https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/  
[2] New York Film Academy, Workshops and campuses: https://www.nyfa.edu/workshops/  
[3] New York Film Academy, Digital Web and Mobile Privacy Policy: https://www.nyfa.edu/privacy-policy/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the institution as New York Film Academy (NYFA) and clearly connect the privacy policy to the same entity described in the filmmaking context (DEEP logic passes for both). In terms of WIDTH/completeness, both cover voluntary PII submission, automatic non-PII data collection, cookies and management, targeted advertising, security safeguards (including SSL/encryption), and provide the privacy contact email. However, Agent A explicitly states that PII is not sold and provides fuller detail about third-party sharing and contact information (including mailing address and phone), while Agent B omits the explicit "not sold" language and provides slightly less comprehensive detail overall. On presentation, Agent A is more scannable and structured with clear bullet sections, making it easier for users to locate specific policy elements quickly. Therefore, Agent A provides a better overall user experience. The loss is categorized as WIDE because Agent B missed a specific checklist detail (explicit non-sale of PII), not due to entity confusion.
